Q1: I organized my study routine primarily around listening to lectures and recorded materials, since I learn best through audio. I'd usually dedicate about three to four hours daily across the week, often with podcasts or lecture recordings playing while I took notes. Honestly, I tried to keep things structured, but sometimes the sadness I've been carrying made it hard to stick to the plan. I'd set up study blocks in my calendar, but my actual engagement varied a lot depending on how I was feeling emotionally that day.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied more than I'd like to admit. I tried to maintain regular study times, but when deadlines approached, everything became more intense and chaotic. The stress really built up during those periods, even though I managed to perform well on exams. Some weeks I'd study consistently, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until pressure forced me to focus.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I needed to check announcements or see if new lecture recordings were posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what assignments were coming up, though I'll admit I didn't always complete them right away. Notifications on my phone would remind me, and that helped, but honestly, logging in felt more like a chore than something I looked forward to.
Q4: There were brief periods where checking the LMS became somewhat routine, usually at the start of the semester when everything felt fresh. But that habit faded as the weeks went on and stress accumulated. The mindfulness exercises I tried helped me stay grounded sometimes, but they didn't really change my LMS habits much. It never truly became automatic for me.
Q5: The recorded lectures and audio materials were absolutely the most useful for me since I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them multiple times, pause, rewind, and really absorb the information at my own pace. Discussion forums were helpful too when other students explained concepts in different ways. Hearing different perspectives through those posts genuinely deepened my understanding.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials and PDF documents because they just don't stick for me the way audio does. I'd download them with good intentions but rarely actually read through them properly. Some of the supplementary resources felt redundant when I'd already understood the concept from lectures. I know my assignment completion rate shows this pattern pretty clearly.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to exam content or when it genuinely helped clarify something confusing. If I could listen to it or discuss it with peers, even better. Activities that felt isolated or purely busywork drained me, especially when I was already struggling emotionally. I needed to feel like my effort would actually pay off.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford, mentally speaking. Required ones had external pressure that pushed me through even on bad days, but optional ones required internal motivation I didn't always have. When I was feeling low, optional activities were the first things I'd skip. There's something about not having a deadline that made them feel less urgent, even if they might have been valuable.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone during commutes or while doing other things. I used external YouTube videos and podcasts to supplement topics I found difficult. My notes were all handwritten, and I'd review those extensively before exams without ever logging in.
Q10: Many times I'd be deeply engaged with the material but completely offline. I'd spend hours listening to downloaded audio files, discussing concepts with friends in person, or just thinking through problems while walking. The LMS wouldn't capture any of that, but those were often my most productive learning moments. My exam scores reflect learning that happened largely outside the platform.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd access a lecture video once, download the audio, and then never watch it on the platform again. I preferred having materials on my devices so I could study anywhere without needing internet. This meant my LMS video watch time was pretty low even though I actually consumed the content multiple times offline.
Q12: There were definitely periods when engagement became really difficult, usually when my sadness felt heavier than usual. Mid-semester was particularly tough when assignments piled up and I felt overwhelmed. The stress would spike, and even though I wasn't anxious exactly, I just felt emotionally exhausted. Those were the times when even logging into the LMS felt like too much effort.
Q13: Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars definitely affected my LMS usage. When I was stressed, I'd prioritize what absolutely had to be done and let other things slide. Fatigue made it hard to engage meaningfully with online materials, so I'd sometimes just skim through things without really absorbing them. The workload felt manageable overall, but my emotional state made it feel heavier than it probably was.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open materials and just stare at them without really processing anything. My mind would wander, especially during periods when I felt sad or disconnected. I'd technically be logged in and have a video playing, but I wasn't truly there mentally. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actual learning.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was the emotional weight I was carrying making it hard to start things. I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I felt better, but that better feeling didn't always come. The irony is that procrastinating only added to my stress, creating this cycle I struggled to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main driver of my LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active, cramming in whatever I needed to complete. Without that external pressure, I'd drift and lose focus easily. My login patterns probably show clear spikes right before due dates and quieter periods in between.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, though sometimes not in the healthiest ways. Positive feedback motivated me to engage more, while critical feedback sometimes made me want to withdraw. When I felt sad, negative feedback hit harder than it should have. I appreciated detailed feedback that helped me improve, but I needed time to process it before I could act on it.
Q18: The course structure influenced my participation significantly. When modules were clearly organized with audio components, I engaged more readily. But when the structure felt confusing or heavily text-based, I struggled to stay motivated. Having clear weekly expectations helped, though I didn't always meet them despite understanding what was required.
Q19: The availability of recorded lectures made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. However, the heavy emphasis on written assignments made things harder since that's not my strength. The discussion forums were well-designed and encouraged peer interaction, which I appreciated. More flexibility in assignment formats would have helped me engage more consistently.
Q20: I think students might be exploring, trying to understand what's expected, or struggling to translate their learning into completed work. Sometimes you're genuinely engaged with understanding the material but can't quite produce the output required. Emotional struggles can also create a gap between effort and results. High activity might reflect genuine learning that just doesn't convert to submissions.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some extent. My exam score was strong despite relatively low LMS engagement because I learned effectively through methods the system couldn't track. Listening to downloaded materials, discussing with peers face-to-face, and personal reflection contributed more to my performance than my login frequency would suggest. The LMS activity doesn't capture the full picture of how I learned.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when students are most active, which resources they access, and whether they're meeting deadlines. But they miss the quality of engagement, the emotional state behind the activity, and all the offline learning that happens. They can't capture whether someone was truly focused or just going through motions. The numbers tell a story, but it's an incomplete one.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's about feeling connected to what you're learning and wanting to explore further. For me, engagement happens when I'm actively listening, questioning, and making connections. It's a mental and emotional investment that goes beyond just showing up or logging in.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally throughout the course, or how the mindfulness interventions helped me cope even if they didn't change my login habits. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to materials offline or the meaningful conversations with classmates that deepened my understanding. My sadness, my stress, my genuine effort to learn despite everything—none of that shows up in the numbers.
